product: Next Level Racing Flight Simulator (Microsoft Flight Simulator Edition)
whyHot: Official Microsoft Flight Simulator partnership rig launched 2025-26 with the industry's first flight-tuned integrated lumbar seat; the reference 'buy the whole cockpit' answer forums point new commercial-flyers to.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero seating/rig entries. This is the aspirational anchor for anyone graduating from a desk clamp to a dedicated cockpit — huge basket-builder and high ticket.

product: Next Level Racing Flight Simulator Lite (NLR-S022)
whyHot: The folding, apartment-friendly rig repeatedly recommended for people with limited space who still want a rigid cockpit-style seat instead of a desk clamp.
notesForCatalog: The affordable/foldable entry into the seating category — the realistic first-rig purchase for most simmers who can't dedicate a room.

product: SimFab Modular Flight Simulator Cockpit (DCS / Rotorcraft Edition)
whyHot: Community-favorite modular rig with pre-drilled brackets that accept Thrustmaster Warthog, WinWing, VIRPIL, VKB and Komodo hardware, and upgrades between fixed-wing, heli and space configs — the flexible sleeper pick vs the big brands.
notesForCatalog: For the builder who wants one frame that flexes across fixed-wing/heli/space and mounts any brand of hardware. Pairs with the collective bracket below.

whyHot: The go-to add-on bracket for mounting a WinWing / VIRPIL / Komodo collective to a rig — solves the perennial 'how do I mount my collective' question in the heli community.
notesForCatalog: Accessory that unlocks the (expensive) collective purchase — catalog has a collective but no way to mount it. Attach-rate item.

product: MonsterTech MTSIM Joystick / HOTAS Table Mount
whyHot: The universally cited premium desk-clamp: BESSEY quick-release clamp rated to 2500 N, anodized aluminum, infinitely height-adjustable — the answer to 'my base slides during aggressive maneuvers.'
notesForCatalog: Catalog has no mounting solutions at all. This is the single most-recommended way to get a stick/throttle off a wobbly desk without buying a full rig — a very common upgrade.

product: Hikig / J-PEIN HOTAS Desk Clamp Pair
whyHot: The budget desk-clamp repeatedly recommended as the 'good enough for a fraction of MonsterTech' entry to getting hardware off the desk surface.
notesForCatalog: Value-tier mounting for cost-conscious builders; broadens the mount category with an entry price point below MonsterTech.

whyHot: Called the first headset where cockpit instruments 'finally come into focus' — 3840x3840 per eye, 50 PPD; the 2026 clarity benchmark that flight simmers rave about for reading gauges without leaning in.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero VR. VR is arguably THE immersion upgrade for flight sim. This is the halo product for enthusiasts with an RTX 4090/5090.

whyHot: At 107g the lightest PCVR headset made; Micro-OLED true blacks win praise for night landings and long-haul legibility. The comfort-first pick for multi-hour flights that the community keeps recommending.
notesForCatalog: The 'comfort king' VR pick — long flights make weight the #1 complaint, and this solves it. Distinct buyer from the Crystal Super.

whyHot: The default budget/accessible entry into VR flight — every 'should I get VR' thread lands here first; great for VFR even if small cockpit text is a stretch. Huge install base.
notesForCatalog: The most-bought VR headset overall and the on-ramp for most simmers. Highest-volume VR entry — anchors the category's affordable end.

product: BOBOVR M3 Pro Head Strap with Battery (Quest 3)
whyHot: The near-universal first Quest 3 upgrade — reviewers and simmers swap the OEM strap for the BOBOVR to fix weight balance and add hot-swap battery for long flights.
notesForCatalog: Perfect high-margin attach item to any Quest 3 sale; directly solves the #1 Quest 3 complaint (comfort/battery). Almost everyone buys one.

whyHot: The plug-and-play tactile-feedback kit (transducer + amp) that flight simmers pair with SimShaker for Aviators to feel gear thumps, stall buffet and touchdown — repeatedly called a high-ROI immersion upgrade.
notesForCatalog: Tactile immersion is completely absent from the catalog. Bundled kit is the easiest sell — no wiring knowledge required. Cross-sells to sim racing too.

product: Dayton Audio BST-300EX 300W Tactile Bass Shaker
whyHot: The DIY community's go-to transducer for 4-corner seat/rig builds — 300W RMS, the unit experienced builders pair with a cheap Nobsound/mono amp for far more punch per dollar than a branded kit.
notesForCatalog: The value/DIY tactile pick — pairs with the amp below. Appeals to the tinkerer segment that scoffs at ButtKicker pricing.

product: Nobsound NS-01G PRO Mini Amplifier (for bass shakers)
whyHot: The cheap Class-D amp everyone recommends to drive a bare Dayton/Douk shaker on a sim rig — the missing piece that makes the DIY tactile build actually work.
notesForCatalog: Completes the DIY tactile trio (shaker + amp + software). Small ticket but essential attach — nobody buys a bare shaker without an amp.

whyHot: The telemetry-driven software that turns a bass shaker into aircraft-specific feedback (gear, flaps, touchdown, stall) for MSFS, X-Plane, DCS, IL-2 — the piece that makes tactile hardware meaningful for flight (not just racing).
notesForCatalog: Software attach that justifies every tactile hardware sale. Even at low affiliate value it strengthens the 'complete tactile setup' story on-page.

whyHot: The long-standing head-tracking standard for non-VR simmers; still the default recommendation for looking around the cockpit and checking six in DCS without a headset.
notesForCatalog: Already in catalog (flight-extra). Listed here to confirm head-tracking is the non-VR immersion path and to anchor the category next to Tobii.

whyHot: WinWing single-handedly crashed the price of high-fidelity Airbus glareshield hardware; the FCU+EFIS combo is the centerpiece of nearly every affordable A320 home-cockpit build in 2026.
notesForCatalog: Already in catalog. Confirms WinWing panels are the hot brand; keep prominent and cross-link to the MIP MCDU/CDU units.

product: WinWing (WINCTRL) OVHD32 SOP Airbus Overhead Panel
whyHot: Announced at FSWeekend 2026 — the overhead panel simmers have been begging for; completes the Airbus home-cockpit stack (FCU/EFIS/MCDU already existed) and is generating major buzz.
notesForCatalog: Brand-new 2026 release, ships June 2026. Overhead panels are a category the catalog lacks entirely — the missing piece of the full-Airbus-cockpit story.

whyHot: Billed as the world's first DIRECT-DRIVE force-feedback joystick base for flight sim; the single biggest hardware story of FSWeekend 2026 and the category everyone says is the next revolution after the MOZA/VPForce wave.
notesForCatalog: Ships late April 2026. Force feedback is the trending frontier; catalog has a MOZA FFB yoke but no FFB stick. This is the buzz product to lead with.

whyHot: The affordable entry into force feedback at FSWeekend 2026 — brings 'pro-grade FFB to the consumer market at competitive prices'; the value door into a category that used to start near $1k.
notesForCatalog: Value FFB tier — the realistic first FFB purchase. Distinct price/buyer from the DB16 direct-drive flagship.

whyHot: MOZA's 12Nm FFB flagship; the other half of the 2026 force-feedback arms race the community obsesses over, cross-compatible with MOZA's racing ecosystem.
notesForCatalog: Gives the FFB category a second brand and lets MOZA-ecosystem owners (who may already have a MOZA racing base) cross-shop. Trending head-to-head with CyberTaurus.

whyHot: The 2026 best-value combat stick everyone recommends: metal build, 6 axes, 26 buttons, handles photogrammetry approaches cleanly at the budget tier — the new default 'first good stick.'
notesForCatalog: The most-recommended budget stick of 2026 and pairs with the Ursa Minor Fighter Throttle. Catalog's sticks are Thrustmaster/Turtle Beach — WinWing is the trending value gap.

whyHot: Perennial community 'best mid-range stick' — ball-bearing gimbal with smooth precise center that both fighter jocks and helicopter pilots single out.
notesForCatalog: The evergreen enthusiast stick recommendation, and the go-to for heli cyclic feel. Catalog has no VKB stick — clear brand gap.

whyHot: The precision benchmark rudder pedals the whole community defers to — composite + aluminum with 17 bearings and magnetic sensors; the 'endgame pedals' answer in every pedal thread.
notesForCatalog: Catalog pedals are Honeycomb/Logitech/Turtle Beach/VKB — no MFG. The aspirational pedal upgrade the community treats as the gold standard.

product: WinWing (WINCTRL) CyberTaurus EB195 Force Feedback Pedals
whyHot: New at FSWeekend 2026 — force-feedback rudder pedals (195N) extending the FFB revolution to the feet; novel category with real buzz for realistic anti-torque/crosswind feel.
notesForCatalog: Ships late April 2026. A genuinely new sub-category (FFB pedals) — differentiates the catalog's pedal lineup with cutting-edge tech.

whyHot: The reference helicopter control set — Asobo/Microsoft used it in MSFS 2024 heli reveals; folding, all-metal cyclic + collective + anti-torque pedals. The definitive heli answer as MSFS 2024 pushed helicopters mainstream.
notesForCatalog: Helicopters are surging in MSFS 2024. Catalog has one Komodo collective but no complete heli set or cyclic. This is the halo heli product.

product: VIRPIL VPC Rotor TCS Plus Collective (with Hawk-60 Grip)
whyHot: The enthusiast/DCS heli community's premium modular collective — adjustable friction and swappable grips; the 'did I make the right choice — yes' pick in MSFS heli-building threads.
notesForCatalog: Second collective option for the serious heli builder; VIRPIL is a marquee brand entirely absent from the catalog.

whyHot: The affordable, only-30mm-thick, plug-and-play CDU that undercuts the big panel brands and works with PMDG 737/777 in MSFS, X-Plane and P3D — a genuine value/sleeper the community trades notes on.
notesForCatalog: A budget alternative to WinWing MCDU for Boeing builders — widens the panel category down-market and is Amazon-affiliate friendly (in-stock, US listings).

whyHot: 1:1 Boeing 737 Mode Control Panel, standalone plug-and-play via Bridge software — the dedicated MCP the 737 home-cockpit crowd points to for real autopilot muscle memory.
notesForCatalog: Boeing-side counterpart to the WinWing Airbus FCU — catalog is Airbus-heavy on glareshield; this fills the Boeing autopilot-panel gap.

whyHot: Turns any tablet/touchscreen into interactive instrument panels and switch panels for MSFS 2020/2024, X-Plane and P3D — the software backbone of DIY glass-cockpit and touchscreen builds the community relies on.
notesForCatalog: Bridges cheap hardware (a spare tablet/monitor) into a working panel — a foundational 'software as gear' entry that supports the touchscreen-panel story.

product: Aviation Kneeboard / Gooseneck EFB Tablet Mount (for iPad Navigraph/SimBrief)
whyHot: MSFS 2024's built-in EFB now streams to a real iPad over Wi-Fi (Navigraph + SimBrief), driving demand for a desk/rig tablet mount to run charts alongside the sim — an active 'how do I mount my tablet' forum topic.
notesForCatalog: Newly relevant because MSFS 2024's EFB now works on a real iPad. Cheap, high-attach accessory; catalog has no EFB/tablet-mount category at all.

whyHot: The community consensus for non-VR immersion: triple 1440p gives ~180° horizontal FOV for VFR scanning while staying within MSFS 2024 VRAM/perf budgets — recommended over ultrawide because ultrawide distorts side references.
notesForCatalog: Displays are entirely missing from the catalog. Triple-monitor is the mainstream non-VR immersion path; frame it as a curated 'best flight-sim monitor' pick rather than one SKU.

product: 49" Curved Super-Ultrawide (32:9) Monitor for Flight Sim
whyHot: The single-screen immersion alternative for those who don't want triple-bezel hassle; 32:9 wraps the cockpit view and is a frequent 'one big screen vs triples' debate winner for IFR/airliner flyers.
notesForCatalog: Alternative display path to triples — one panel, no bezels, easier setup. Broadens the (missing) monitor category with a second buyer profile.

experienceRole: compute / GPU for demanding sim engine
whyHot: The balanced high-end GPU pick for MSFS 2024 — holds 4K Ultra with frame-gen and is the right card for high-end VR at sensible settings; the most-recommended 'don't need a 5090' answer in 2026 build threads.
notesForCatalog: MSFS 2024 is the most GPU-hungry sim; the PC/GPU angle is missing entirely. The 5080 is the enthusiast sweet spot — great buyer-guide anchor.

experienceRole: compute / flagship GPU for VR + triples
whyHot: The only card that comfortably clears high-resolution VR (Pimax Crystal Super) and photogrammetry-dense MSFS 2024 at once; the endgame GPU every high-fidelity VR flyer covets despite scarce stock.
notesForCatalog: The halo GPU that makes the halo VR headsets usable. Pairs narratively with the Pimax Crystal Super entry — 'what you need to actually run it.'

whyHot: Home cockpits chew through USB ports (yoke, throttle, pedals, panels, MCDU, head-tracker, shaker); a powered hub is the unglamorous must-have every multi-panel builder ends up buying to stop device dropouts.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already has a Desktop Pilot 10-port hub. Confirmed as a foundational accessory — keep it and cross-sell alongside every panel.

product: Cable Sleeve / Velcro Tie + Under-Desk Tray Kit (flight-sim cockpit wiring)
whyHot: Multi-panel cockpits become a cable nest; wiring-tidy kits are a recurring 'finally tamed my setup' post — low-cost, high-satisfaction, and a natural bundle add to any hub/panel purchase.
notesForCatalog: Cable management is entirely absent. Cheap, universal, high-attach — ideal 'complete your setup' upsell. Frame as a curated kit rather than one SKU.

product: Gaming Headset with Clear Mic for VATSIM/ATC (e.g. HyperX / SteelSeries class)
whyHot: Online ATC networks (VATSIM/PilotEdge) and MSFS 2024 live ATC made a clear-mic headset an immersion staple; the community steers newcomers to a comfortable closed-back set with a good boom mic for radio calls.
notesForCatalog: Audio/comms is a missing category. As live/online ATC grows, a clear-mic headset is a genuine need and a high-volume affiliate item. Frame as curated pick.
